projects.title: Neural Notes — AI Search That Understands Meaning
projects.tagline: Semantic note search running 100% in your browser — no server, no API key, your notes never leave the tab.

projects.title: Mood of the Room — Per-Sentence Sentiment in Your Browser
projects.tagline: Paste any text or chat export and watch its emotional temperature light up sentence by sentence — 100% client-side, no API key.
projects.signal: transformers.js v4 released on NPM (browser-ML week momentum)
projects.rationale: Per-sentence heatmap turns a flat whole-doc score into a visible emotional arc — fast 10s demo; won the day-2 slot as the backlog item on a tie with the signal-derived idea.

projects.title: Zero-Shot Tagger — Classify Anything Into Your Own Labels
projects.tagline: Invent labels, paste text, get an instant classification with no training data — a zero-shot NLI model running 100% in your browser.
projects.rationale: Highest slate score (11/12): user-defined labels with zero training is a genuinely distinctive in-browser wow that few ship client-side.

projects.title: Token Cost Lab — See What a Prompt Actually Costs
projects.tagline: Paste a prompt, pick your models, and watch the per-request cost light up across GPT-5.6, Claude Sonnet 5, Grok 4.5 and more — tokenized 100% in your browser, no API key.
projects.signal: Grok 4.5 launched 2026-07-09 + GPT-5.6 Terra/Luna GA mid-July 2026; new price tiers churning this week
projects.rationale: Top slate score (11/12): rides live model-pricing news, 10-second paste-and-see-cost demo, and a genuinely distinctive tokenizer-multiplier angle (Sonnet 5's ~42% token inflation) that shows real depth.

projects.title: Similar or Not — Watch Meaning Become Geometry
projects.tagline: Type a handful of sentences and see them embed, cluster on a 2D map, and light up a cosine-similarity heatmap — an embeddings playground running 100% in your browser.
projects.rationale: Catch-up second build (2 days behind). Reliable, visual transformers.js embeddings demo that pairs a from-scratch power-iteration PCA with a cosine heatmap; adds topic variety (tokenizer + embeddings) alongside the day-4 winner.

projects.title: SkillScan — X-ray an AI Agent Skill Before You Install It
projects.tagline: Paste any agent skill file and get an instant security report: prompt injection, data exfiltration, dangerous commands, permission creep and context cost — 100% in your browser.
projects.signal: Agent-skill supply chain is this week's story: GitHub trending is stacked with installable skills (obra/superpowers, safishamsi/graphify, JuliusBrussee/caveman, openai/codex-plugin-cc, ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp) — all distributed as 'drop this markdown into your agent' with no review step. usestrix/strix (AI pentest) trending alongside signals the security counter-current.
projects.rationale: Only perfect 12/12 of the run. Rides the hottest live trend with the angle nobody has shipped — everyone is publishing skills, nobody is auditing them. Demo-ability is exceptional (opens on a hostile fixture, grade F in one second, zero setup), and it positions Bipul as the security-minded adult in the agent-skills room — the single most Director-shaped stance available this week.

projects.title: MCP Auditor — Audit Your MCP Server Config Before Your Agent Does
projects.tagline: Paste your Claude Desktop / Cursor / VS Code mcpServers config and get an instant security report: hardcoded secrets, unpinned remote execution, root filesystem access, and dangerous capability combos like filesystem+network exfiltration paths — 100% in your browser.
projects.signal: ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp still trending today (404 stars today) alongside agentskills/agentskills going trending as the new formal Agent Skills spec — MCP tool sprawl remains a live, undernourished story. This exact idea was appended to the backlog on day 6 after losing to SkillScan at 9/12; today's rescan puts it back on top.
projects.rationale: Highest score in today's slate (10/12: Positioning 3, Timeliness 2, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 2) — beat the two next backlog items (Lingua Detect, Ask My Docs) and two other fresh signal ideas (a skill-spec conformance linter riding today's agentskills/agentskills trend, and a Foundry model comparison board riding Claude-on-Azure-Foundry news), both of which scored 8/12 and risked overlapping with prior builds (SkillScan and Token Cost Lab respectively). MCP Auditor is the direct sibling to Day 006's SkillScan — same rule-engine architecture, same scoring model — completing a two-day 'AI agent supply-chain security' arc: Day 6 audited the skills an agent loads, Day 7 audits the servers it talks to. Coherent, Director-shaped, and rides a signal that is still fresh two days later.

projects.title: Prompt Router — Stop Sending Every Prompt to Your Most Expensive Model
projects.tagline: Paste a day's worth of prompts and watch each one get routed to the cheapest model tier that can actually handle it — an embedding + feature classifier running 100% in your browser, with a live savings ledger.
projects.signal: CNBC reports 30-46% of US enterprise AI token spend is already flowing to cheap Chinese models; Z.ai's open-weights GLM-5.2 (list $1.40/$4.40 per 1M) is being reported as matching frontier models on long-horizon coding at a fraction of the price, and a 1.6T-param Chinese model was open-sourced under MIT this week. GPT-5.6 Luna/Terra hit GA and Grok 4.5 launched 2026-07-08, so the price ladder itself is churning.
projects.rationale: Top of a 5-candidate slate (11/12: Positioning 3, Timeliness 3, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 2). The week's real story is not 'a new model shipped' but 'cheap models became good enough' — which turns model *selection* into an engineering problem, and that is exactly the AI/ML-Director conversation. Beat the two next backlog items (Lingua Detect 6/12, Ask My Docs 9/12) and tied with a signal-derived 'Open Model Passport' idea (11/12) — broke the tie toward Prompt Router because it does real in-browser ML inference rather than being a third static rule-engine in a row after SkillScan and MCP Auditor, and because a live savings ledger is a harder demo to look away from. Distinctiveness marked down to 2 (not 3) honestly: the cost framing is adjacent to Day 4's Token Cost Lab, though the product is genuinely different — Day 4 prices a prompt, Day 8 decides where to send it.

projects.title: Open Model Passport — Can You Actually Ship This Model?
projects.tagline: Pick an AI model, declare what you intend to do with it, and get an instant shipping-clearance report — licence, weights, residency and EU AI Act exposure, with a source link on every claim.
projects.signal: Xiaomi's MiMo-V2-Pro is reported as the most-used model on OpenRouter by weekly token volume (~21.1% platform share, 4.21T weekly tokens, vs OpenAI's 7.5%) — yet its weights are NOT released (API-only), despite Xiaomi's open-weight commitment and its MIT-licensed MiMo-V2-Flash predecessor. Governance is this week's drumbeat: China's CAC companion-AI rules take effect TODAY (2026-07-15), with ByteDance/Alibaba giving Qwen agent users a same-day export deadline and no migration path; the UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance met in Geneva 6-7 Jul 2026; and trade press notes 'regulators picked a word for AI this week, and the word is systemic'.
projects.rationale: Only 12/12 of the slate (Positioning 3, Timeliness 3, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 3). The week's story is that the model everyone is actually running is one nobody can actually download — the gap between what a model is CALLED and what you can legally ship is exactly the question an IT Director signs off on, and almost nobody has shipped a tool for it (everyone benchmarks quality and price instead). Beat the two next backlog items (Lingua Detect 4/12 — tutorial-shaped, no signal; Ask My Docs 8/12 — WebLLM's multi-GB first load undercuts the 10-second wow) and two fresh signal ideas: a 'blast radius' agent-command simulator riding Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard trending at 473 stars today (11/12, lost on Distinctiveness — it would have been the third agent-security rule engine after SkillScan and MCP Auditor), and Prompt Compressor (8/12, stale meme). Carried over from the day-8 slate where it tied at 11/12 and lost the tie-break; today's governance signals push Timeliness to a clean 3. Design keeps it honest: every fact carries source + asOf + verified/reported confidence, and undisclosed training compute returns an explicit 'cannot determine' finding rather than a silent pass — 8 of 9 catalogued models publish no FLOP figure at all.

projects.title: Blast Radius — Simulate What That Command Destroys
projects.tagline: Paste the command your agent wants to run and watch it destroy a virtual machine instead of yours — what dies, what comes back, and a safer way to say it.
projects.signal: Agent skills own GitHub trending today and the ecosystem has gone mainstream-aesthetic rather than safety-conscious: Nutlope/hallmark ('anti-AI-slop design skill') is #2 at 3,372 stars today, mattpocock/skills 2,060, ibelick/ui-skills 178, Graphify-Labs/graphify 1,107 — four of the top 15 repos are 'drop this markdown in and let it run commands', with no review step anywhere. github/copilot-sdk trending alongside signals the same install-an-agent motion reaching the Microsoft stack. Carried from the day-9 slate where it scored 11/12 riding Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard (473 stars).
projects.rationale: Tied at 11/12 with the fresh Slop Radar idea (Positioning 3, Timeliness 2, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 3) and won the tie as the backlog item, per the selection protocol. Timeliness marked down to 2 honestly — its originating signal is two days old — but the underlying trend got stronger, not weaker: the skills ecosystem is now optimising for taste while the thing those skills actually do is run shell commands unreviewed. Distinctiveness earns a clean 3: this is a visual simulator, not a fourth rule-engine report card after SkillScan (day 6) and MCP Auditor (day 7) — it expands the command, walks a virtual filesystem, and scores on recoverability rather than scariness, so 'rm -rf node_modules' correctly reads as fine while 'rm -rf scratch/*' — a hundredth of the bytes — reads as destructive. Beat Ask My Docs (7/12) and Lingua Detect (4/12) from the backlog and Context Rot Lab (9/12, feasibility-risky).

projects.title: Prompt Compressor — What Token Savings Actually Cost You
projects.tagline: The most-starred AI coding skill on GitHub promises 65% fewer tokens. Paste your own text and measure what it actually saves — and what meaning it quietly drops.
projects.signal: JuliusBrussee/caveman is #3 on GitHub trending at 82,764 stars (+2,851 today) — the most-starred AI coding agent skill on GitHub — advertising a 65% token cut by talking like a caveman. JetBrains published a measurement this month putting the real saving at 8.5% on agentic tasks, because the 65% is measured on chat prose while agent traffic is code/diffs/traces that any honest compressor leaves verbatim. Context economics is the week's live theme from both ends: Gemini 3.5 Pro launched 2026-07-17 with a 2M-token window at $1.25/M in, and Moonshot's Kimi K3 (2.8T MoE, 1M context, open weights promised 2026-07-27) landed 2026-07-16 — windows are exploding while the #3 trending repo is a hack to stop filling them.
projects.rationale: Only 12/12 of the slate (Positioning 3, Timeliness 3, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 3). The story isn't that caveman's 65% is a lie — it reproduces (my chat-prose fixture lands at 50.9%). It's that the number was measured on a distribution nobody actually sends, then generalised: benchmark-vs-reality is the exact judgement an IT Director is paid to make, and it's the single most Director-shaped stance available against the week's most-starred repo. Beat the two next backlog items (Ask My Docs 7/12 — WebLLM's multi-GB first load still kills the 10-second wow; Lingua Detect 4/12 — tutorial-shaped, no signal) and two fresh signal ideas: AI Risk Tier Classifier at 9/12 (rides WAICO — China's 29-nation AI alliance launched at the Shanghai World AI Conference today with Xi's first-ever keynote and the UN SG attending — but overlaps Day 9's Open Model Passport governance ground) and a Skill Portability Checker at 8/12 (rides agentskills/agentskills + openai/codex-plugin-cc + herdr, but would have been the third rule-engine report card after SkillScan and MCP Auditor). Carried from the day-4 slate where it scored 10/12 and lost to Token Cost Lab on cost-framing overlap; the JetBrains measurement is what changed it — this is no longer a cost calculator (Day 4 prices a prompt, Day 8 routes it) but a claim-verification instrument whose primary output is a fidelity-vs-savings trade-off, i.e. an ML measurement, so Distinctiveness earns a clean 3. Demo proves itself in one second: opens on an agent-task fixture showing advertised 65% struck through next to a real 24.1%, with the composition bar explaining exactly why. Kept honest deliberately — the fixture reports 24.1%, not the scarier 8.5%, because a user's task message genuinely is more prose-heavy than a full agent transcript, and the README says so rather than padding the fixture with code to manufacture a bigger gap.

projects.title: Context Window Packer — Fit the Best Context Into N Tokens
projects.tagline: Inkling just shipped a 1M-token window — but you still can't afford to fill it with junk. Paste your sources, set a token budget, and watch a 0/1-knapsack packer choose the highest-value context that fits.
projects.signal: Thinking Machines' Inkling (Mira Murati) shipped ~Jul 15 as a ~1T-param open-weight multimodal model with a 1,000,000-token context window (weights on Hugging Face); TechCrunch Jul 14 'the real AI race may no longer be at the frontier' as Chinese open models hit 41% of HF downloads.
projects.rationale: Top slate score (11/12). Inkling's 1M-token window makes 'how you pack the window' the live question this week — a Week-2 RAG backlog idea (context-packer) elevated by a <7-day signal. Beats chunk-lab (9) and foundry-model-board (8) on Timeliness+Demo: real 0/1-knapsack DP vs naive truncation is a 10-second visual wow and shows genuine algorithmic depth, not another comparison dashboard.

projects.title: Hybrid Search — BM25 + Vectors, Fused with RRF
projects.tagline: Watch a keyword ranker and a semantic ranker disagree on the same query, then fuse them with Reciprocal Rank Fusion into a list that is rarely wrong on either — 100% in your browser, no API key.
projects.signal: Context windows exploded again this week — Moonshot Kimi K3 (2.8T, released 2026-07-16) and Thinking Machines' Inkling (~1T open-weight, ~2026-07-15) both ship 1M-token windows, and the week's meta-narrative is 'best fit wins, not best model' (GPT-5.6 Sol/Terra/Luna + Grok 4.5 price war). The reflex is 'just retrieve everything into the giant window' — which makes retrieval QUALITY, not window size, the live question. Azure's HorizonDB (built-in vector indexing) landed the same week from the Microsoft side.
projects.rationale: Top of a 5-candidate slate (10/12: Positioning 3, Timeliness 2, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 2). Tied at 10 with a fresh signal-derived 'RAG Injection Scanner' (rides usestrix/strix #1 on GitHub trending today, ~2.8k stars) and won the tie as the backlog item per the selection protocol — and, deliberately, because a RAG-engineering build keeps this week's Week-2 arc coherent (Day 12 context-packer -> Day 13 retrieval) instead of shipping a 4th paste-and-get-a-report-card security tool after SkillScan/MCP-Auditor/Blast-Radius. Beat Reranker Demo (9/12, cross-encoder model download is a feasibility/demo risk), Chunk Lab (8/12) and a price-war 'Best-Fit Model Picker' (7/12, overlaps Day 4 Token Cost Lab + Day 8 Prompt Router). The build ships real depth: hand-written Okapi BM25 (Lucene +1 idf), MiniLM cosine, and Reciprocal Rank Fusion on rank (k=60) — with a curated adversarial corpus where BM25 and the vectors genuinely disagree so fusion visibly earns its keep. RAG Injection Scanner (>=8) appended to the backlog.

projects.title: Bhasha Detect — Identify India's 22 Scheduled Languages In Your Browser
projects.tagline: Paste text in any of India's 22 scheduled languages — plus Hinglish — and watch it get identified by Unicode script and character n-grams, with an honest confusion-matrix explorer. 100% client-side, no API key.
projects.signal: India flagship day (day 14 — pool-only slate). Live signal: BharatGen's sovereign-AI milestone of covering all 22 scheduled Indian languages is landing now (targeted mid-2026), alongside Sarvam's 30B/105B and Gnani.ai's 5B voice model — India's differentiation is language coverage, not raw scale, while the global frontier commoditises (Inkling ~1T open-weight Jul 15, Kimi K3 Jul 16).
projects.rationale: Only 12/12 of the India Flagship Pool slate (Positioning 3, Timeliness 3, Demo-ability 3, Distinctiveness 3). It is the definitional 'AI voice of India' build: a client-side identifier for all 22 Eighth-Schedule languages + Hinglish, riding the exact BharatGen-22-languages milestone in the news. Beat Indic OCR Lab (11, feasibility-flagged: multi-MB runtime traineddata and OCR quality is UNVERIFIABLE without a browser this run), Hinglish Bridge (10, Roman→Devanagari is ambiguous without a model), India AI Pulse (9, 'live/auto-updated' can't be delivered honestly one-run), Kisan Sahayak (9, RAG again — overlaps days 12-13), and UPI Guard (7, CLI/no live wow + sandbox .NET risk). Won partly BECAUSE it is honest: a two-stage script+n-gram design with explicit capability tiers (content-profiled / script-identified / script-only) that shows exactly which of the 22 it can confirm and which it can only narrow to a script — the Director-shaped stance. Deterministic, instant-load, zero model download, and fully Vitest-testable in the sandbox: 88 tests pass and the built-in confusion matrix scores 87.5% leave-one-out (56/64) with every error inside a shared script.

projects.title: Will It Fit? — Can You Actually Run That Open-Weight Model?
projects.tagline: Pick an open-weight LLM (Kimi K3, LongCat-2.0, GPT-OSS…), a quantization, and your GPU or Azure VM — instantly see if it fits in VRAM and how fast it decodes. 100% in your browser, no API key.
projects.signal: Kimi K3 (2.8T, 1M ctx) released 2026-07-16 with open weights promised 27 Jul + LongCat-2.0 (1.6T MoE); AMD 'Advancing AI 2026' conference 22-23 Jul + Microsoft-AMD Helios/EPYC Azure expansion (20 Jul) — 'can I run these giant open models, and on what Azure VM' is this week's live question.
projects.rationale: Perfect 12/12. Opens a brand-new axis for the series — the infra/deployment reality of the Microsoft-stack AI leader — that none of the prior 14 browser-NLP toys touched. Rides two concurrent <7-day signals (Kimi K3 weights drop + AMD/Azure hardware week), demos in <1s with zero model download, and teaches the genuinely misread MoE 'total-vs-active VRAM' gap (weights sized by 2.8T total, decode by 32B active).
